title: A microscopic design rule for spin supersolids in triangular-lattice magnets
authors: Ryota Ono, Jun'ichi Ieda, Michiyasu Mori, Sadamichi Maekawa
abstract: Spin supersolids emerge as a central topic in frustrated magnetism, motivating the search for realization in quantum materials. To this end, we study the origin of exchange anisotropy, $Δ$, in triangular-lattice cobaltate families $X_2$$Y$Co(PO$_4$)$_2$ and $X_2$Co(SeO$_3$)$_2$ ($X$ = Na, K, Rb, Cs; $Y$ = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) by tailoring realistic spin models. We show that $Δ$ is determined by the ratio of trigonal crystal field to spin-orbit coupling strength. This framework explains contrasting anisotropies in these families, predicts systematic trends in $Δ$ across $X/Y$-substitutions, and identifies candidate materials for spin supersolids. Our results establish trigonal field engineering as a microscopic route toward the design of spin supersolids.
summary: This theoretical study develops a microscopic design rule for spin supersolids in triangular-lattice magnets by analyzing cobaltate compounds. It shows that the crucial exchange anisotropy parameter ($\Delta$) is controlled by the ratio of crystal field splitting to spin-orbit coupling strength. This framework allows researchers to predict and engineer materials exhibiting exotic quantum magnetic states.
main_problem: To establish a microscopic design rule for realizing and tuning spin supersolidity in triangular-lattice magnets by understanding the origin of exchange anisotropy ($\Delta$).
main_result: The exchange anisotropy $\Delta$ is determined by the ratio of the trigonal crystal field strength to the spin–orbit coupling strength, allowing systematic prediction across related material families.
method: Calculations combine first-principles electronic structure methods (DFT/cRPA) with strong-coupling superexchange theory and effective low-energy Hubbard model construction.
model_or_system: The system involves $	ext{Co}^{2+}$ ions forming a quasi-two-dimensional triangular lattice in cobaltate families ($	ext{X}_2	ext{Y}	ext{Co}(	ext{PO}_4)_2$ and $	ext{X}_2	ext{Co}(	ext{SeO}_3)_2$). The physics is mapped onto an effective pseudospin-$1/2$ XXZ model.
key_observables: Exchange anisotropy parameter ($\Delta$), single-ion anisotropy factor ($\gamma$), nearest-neighbor exchange couplings ($J_{ab}^{ij}$), and crystal field parameters ($A_{20}, A_{40}$).
important_parameters: Ratio of trigonal crystal field to SOC strength ($\omega_{	ext{trig}}/\lambda_{	ext{eff}}$); $\Delta > 1$ for supersolid; $\Delta < 1$ for superfluid.
main_assumptions: The low-energy physics can be accurately described by projecting the full Hamiltonian onto a Kramers doublet (pseudospin-$1/2$) manifold, and that superexchange theory is applicable.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate crystal structures, energy level schemes for $	ext{Co}^{2+}$, and plots mapping calculated $\Delta$ values across various cation substitutions to identify supersolid candidates.
paper_structure: The paper systematically moves from analyzing the local electronic structure (DFT/cRPA) to deriving an effective low-energy Hubbard model. It then uses superexchange theory to calculate magnetic couplings, finally relating these couplings to structural parameters ($\omega_{	ext{trig}}/\lambda_{	ext{eff}}$) that govern $\Delta$.
why_interesting: The work provides a quantitative, first-principles pathway (crystal field engineering) to tune quantum magnetic phases like supersolidity in solid-state materials, bridging orbital physics with frustrated magnetism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Crystal structure of NaBaCP and KCSO. (b) Energy level scheme of the lowest multiplet of Co2+ in an octahedral crystal field with spin-orbit coupling (SOC). The energy gap between the Jeff = 1/2 ground state doublet and the first excited spin-orbit multiplet is denoted by ∆Eex. (c) triangular-lattice plane formed by CoO6 clusters in NaBaCP and KCSO. The green dashed arrow indicates the bond along the crystallographic b-axis (ϕij = 2π/3).

Figure 2. Plot of ∆as a function of ωtrig/λeff for the target materials. Solid and open circles denote calculated values for the XBaCP and XCSO series, respectively. While solid and open stars indicate experimental estimates for KCSO and NaBaCP, respectively. The solid black curve shows the theoretical relation ∆= γ as a function of ωtrig/λeff obtained by solving the minimal single-ion model in Eq. (5). Above the plot, schematic illustrations show the relation between the trigonal crystal field strength and the local CoO6 distortion in a simplified picture that retains only the contribution of the surrounding O2−ions.

Figure 3. ∆in the XYCP family by combined X- and Y - site substitutions. The red and blue colors denote ∆> 1 and ∆< 1, respectively, corresponding to the supersolid and superfluid of the XXZ phase diagram.
